coredata.dc:title: Using easily prepared carbon nanodots to improve hole transport capacity of perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhiqi
Liu, Chunyu
Zhang, Xinyuan
Guo, Jiaxin
Cui, Houxiao
Shen, Liang
Bi, Yanfeng
Guo, Wenbin
coredata.dc:description: Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) is the most widely-used transparent anode material of commercialization. However, the partially bonding between PSS and PEDOT greatly decreases the structural and electronic homogeneity of PEDOT:PSS system. In view of their molecular structure and functional groups, a S, N co-doped carbon nanodots (CNDs) was developed as ameliorant to prepare PEDOT:PSS:CNDs hole transport layer. The uncombined S double bond introduces more microcosmic interstice inside CNDs, accompanying with many N-rich amino on the surface. This highly reactive products can band the redundant PSS and remove the insulating component from PEDOT:PSS mixture, leading to the conductivity and structural homogeneity improvement. The hole-transport capability of PEDOT:PSS:CNDs is systematically investigated in inverted planar hetero-junction (PHJ) perovskite solar cells (PSCs) fabricated in air, and an optimized power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 18.03% was achieved, which is superior to PEDOT:PSS based PSCs produced under atmospheric. This work offers a new strategy to improve the charge transfer capability of PEDOT:PSS in PSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Easily prepared
PEDOT:PSS:CNDs
Perovskite solar cells
Hole-transport layer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Molecular formula of citric acid and thiourea; (b) ionized citric acid and thiourea; (c) Conformation of the polymer chains; (d) Dehydrolysis formed CNDs with abundant amide groups on the surface, Inset: photographs of CNDs under sunlight (left) and under UV (365 nm) illumination (right); TEM image of CNDs: (e) scale bar: 20 nm, (f) scale bar: 10 nm; (g) HRTEM image of CNDs, scale bar: 5 nm; (h) Crystallite electron diffraction pattern of CNDs; (i) Optical absorption and PL spectra of CNDs at different excitation wavelengths, Inset: photographs of CNDs water solution under sunlight (left) and under UV (365 nm) illumination (right); (j) XRD spectrum of the CNDs; (k) Raman spectra of CNDs.

(a) Chemical structure of PEDOT:PSS; (b) 2D structure for PEDOT:PSS; (c) Photographs of PEDOT:PSS; (d) 3D structure for PEDOT:PSS; (e) Growth mechanism for PEDOT:PSS:CNDs; (f) 2D structure for PEDOT:PSS:CNDs; (g) Photographs of PEDOT:PSS:CNDs solution with CNDs concentration of 5 mg/mL; (h) 3D structure for PEDOT:PSS; (i) The possible internal growth mechanism for CNDs; TEM images of S,N co-doped CNDs after sustained electron bombarding; (j) scale bar: 10 nm and (k) scale bar: 5 nm; (l) Raman spectra of PEDOT:PSS and PEDOT:PSS:CNDs.

SEM cross-sectional image of the device configuration: (a) scale bar: 1 μm, inset: the device structure used in this study, (b) scale bar: 100 nm; (c) J-V curves of PSCs (1 represents PEDOT:PSS device, 2 represents PEDOT:PSS:CNDs device), inset: photovoltaic parameters of the devices; AFM surface morphology image of PEDOT:PSS without and with CNDs: (d) 0 mg/mL, (e) 0.2 mg/mL, (f) 0.3 mg/mL, (g) 0.4 mg/mL; (h) J-V curves of PSCs in dark on semi-log coordinate; (i) J-V curves of PEDOT:PSS:CNDs films, inset: conductivity of PEDOT:PSS:CNDs films; (j) PL decay spectrum of PEDOT:PSS:CNDs films.
(a) The air stability of PSCs, (b) the UV transmittance of PEDOT:PSS:CNDs and PEDOT:PSS; the water contact angles of PEDOT:PSS with CNDs (c) 0 mg/mL, (d) 0.2 mg/mL, (e) 0.3 mg/mL, (f) 0.4 mg/mL.

head.ce:title: Using easily prepared carbon nanodots to improve hole transport capacity of perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Integrated Optoelectronics, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Integrated Optoelectronics
College of Electronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environmental Engineering, Liaoning Shihua University, Fushun 113001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) is the most widely-used transparent anode material of commercialization. However, the partially bonding between PSS and PEDOT greatly decreases the structural and electronic homogeneity of PEDOT:PSS system. In view of their molecular structure and functional groups, a S, N co-doped carbon nanodots (CNDs) was developed as ameliorant to prepare PEDOT:PSS:CNDs hole transport layer. The uncombined S double bond introduces more microcosmic interstice inside CNDs, accompanying with many N-rich amino on the surface. This highly reactive products can band the redundant PSS and remove the insulating component from PEDOT:PSS mixture, leading to the conductivity and structural homogeneity improvement. The hole-transport capability of PEDOT:PSS:CNDs is systematically investigated in inverted planar hetero-junction (PHJ) perovskite solar cells (PSCs) fabricated in air, and an optimized power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 18.03% was achieved, which is superior to PEDOT:PSS based PSCs produced under atmospheric. This work offers a new strategy to improve the charge transfer capability of PEDOT:PSS in PSCs.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic–inorganic hybrid perovskite solar cells (PSCs) are emerging as next-generation photovoltaic cells for their broad absorption, high electron/hole mobility, direct band-gap, and long exciton diffusion length [1–4]. Great progress of power conversion efficiency (PCE) has been achieved in both mesoporous and planar hetero-junction (PHJ) devices [5–7]. Conventional PSCs are typically fabricated using metal oxides (TiO2, ZnO, and SnO2) as the electron transport layer (ETL) [8–10] and 2,2′,7,7′-Tetrakis[N,N-di(4-methoxyphenyl)amino]-9,9′-spiro-bifluorene(spiro-OMeTAD) as the hole transport layer (HTL). [11,12], However, the high prices of spiro-OMeTAD hinder their commercial application, and the high-temperature annealing process of metal oxides also goes against their use on the flexible substrates. To meet the high throughput manufacturing demand, the inverted PHJ PSCs have been developed and demonstrate the promising potential in flexible and low-temperature solution process. [13,14], In inverted devices, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) has become the most widely-used hole extraction material due to its high work function (4.8–5.1 eV), high transparency, and solution preparation [15]. However, the hygroscopic and natural acidity of PEDOT:PSS reduce the air stability and result in the rapid degradation on device performance [16]. The exorbitant conglutination between PEDOT and PSS caused by the linear structure and ultrahigh molecular weight of PSS, commonly decreases the electrical and structural homogeneity. [17,18], To tackle these problems, many practical ways, such as PEDOT based materials synthesis, small-molecule doping, and “dipping” post-treatment have been developed [19–21]. Among them, the molecule doping including ionic liquid, anionic surfactant, inorganic salt, and polar solvents can weaken the coulombic interaction between PEDOT and PSS, leading to the conductivity improvement of PEDOT:PSS film [22–25]. However, the materials mentioned above are regarded as environmentally harmful or high cost. Thus, it remains challenge and desirable to develop an efficient and environment-friendly additive to boost the electrical and structural homogeneity for PEDOT:PSS system.

Carbon nano-dots (CNDs), on the merits of non-toxicity, low-cost, biocompatibility, and water solubility, are becoming a class of novel environmentally-friendly zero-dimensional nano-materials [26–28]. Various chemical structures with corresponding surface modification allow CNDs to be developed for assorted purposes: photocatalytic hydrogen production [29], environmental remediation [30], water splitting [31], and light-triggered theranostics [32]. Especially, the superior optical and electrical properties make it potential candidates for the advancement of sensors [33], energy conversion/storage [34], light emitting diodes [35], and fluorescent probes [36]. Although CNDs have been used in a wide field, the study on PSCs is still in its infancy, and further research is necessary.

CNDs were synthesized by a solvothermal process, using citric acid as carbon source and excess thiourea as sulfur agent (experimental section in support information). As depicted in the procedures schematic of Fig. 1 , the functional amines of thiourea (Fig. 1a) in aqueous solution can be partially protonated by accepting protons (H+) dissociated from the water, which exhibits the cationic property. Simultaneously, the functional hydroxyl and carboxyl of citric acid (Fig. 1a) can be ionized by losing protons (H+) to the water, which indicates the anionic property (Fig. 1b). The positively charged amines (protonated amines) of the thiourea strongly interact with the negatively charged terminal oxygen ions of citric acid and then spontaneously alter the conformation of the polymer chains. Under solvothermal condition, the thiourea and citric acid could assemble into a nanoplate structure through the inter-molecular H-bonding (Fig. 1c). Dehydrolysis process enables to form nitrogen and sulfur-rich CNDs with abundant amide groups on the surface because of excess thiourea (Fig. 1d) [37]. Consequently, the prepared CNDs exhibited a blue color under 365 nm UV light. Elemental analysis in Fig. S1 and XPS in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information) proved the existence of C, S, and N elements.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images in Fig. 1e indicate that the prepared CNDs primarily consist of sphere-like structures in an individual particle with the uniform diameter in a narrow size distribution. TEM image in Fig. 1f and the size distribution histogram in Fig. S3 (Supporting Information) reveal the particle size of CNDs is about 7∼10 nm with a dominant averaged diameter of ∼8.7 ± 0.4 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) in Fig. 1g illustrates the circular shape of CNDs with visible crystalline lattice fringes. Carefully observing, the crystal lattice spacing distances is approximately 0.21 nm, which are consistent with the d-spacing of the (100) lattice spacing of graphene, indicating the graphitic nature of nanoparticle. [38,39], The discernible atomic arrangement and weak rings in the electron diffraction pattern (Fig. 1h) indicate a certain degree of crystallinity, corresponding to the well-resolved crystal lattice structures.

The optical absorption of CNDs (Fig. 1i) shows a near ultraviolet absorption band with a peak at 200 nm, which manifests excellent ultraviolet absorption. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum in Fig. 1i shows an emission band in the region of 400–600 nm, with a strong peak at 425 nm under a 380 nm excitation due to the geminate recombination of localized electron–hole pairs in sp2 clusters. [40,41], The peak shifts a little to the long wavelength and the intensity decreases when the excitation wavelength rises. XRD spectrum in Fig. 1j shows a wide-angle pattern with a broad peak centered at 25.8, which suggests the graphitization and small size of CNDs with ordered carbon atoms [42]. Raman spectra (Fig. 1k) exhibits two intrinsic peaks: 1) the disordered D band peaking at 1350 cm−1 is related to the sp3-hybrids, which reflects the disorder or defects caused by the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite; 2) the crystalline G band peaking at 1580 cm−1 is correlated to the vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice [41,43].

The synthesis of PEDOT:PSS includes three phases: 1) PEDOT is oxidized to be electroneutral PEDOT, which is insulating and insoluble in water; 2) neutral PEDOT is oxidized to lose electrons and forms oxidation type PEDOT with positive charge; 3) positive ions of PEDOT and negative ions of PSS attract each other and form PEDOT:PSS (Fig. 2 a, b and c). However, as shown in Fig. 2b and d, the exorbitant conglutination between PEDOT and PSS would decrease the electrical and structural homogeneity, leading to low system conductivity. For PEDOT:PSS system, sulfonic acid groups of PSS can be partially protonated by losing protons (H+) in the water, which is responsible for the negative ions of PSS. Meanwhile, The S, N co-doped feature of CNDs allows more functional amidogen exposure to the solution, which can be partially ionized in its water solution. It is like a conjugating pathway of metabolic processes in numerous cells, the functional carbon support will tend to associate with the sulfonic acid groups of PSS (Fig. 2e), leaving the hydrophobic PEDOT in the water (Fig. 2f). Thus, the moderate amounts of CNDs can band the superfluous PSS and remove the insulating component from PEDOT:PSS mixture (Fig. S4 in Supporting Information exhibits photographs of PEDOT:PSS:CNDs blend solution in the different proportions), but excessive CNDs results in despoilment of sulfonic acid groups from PEDOT, which will transform uniform liquid into the final layered product (Fig. 2g and h, Supplementary Movie 1) [18,20,21].
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtener.2019.01.002.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Video 1
3
Video 1

N doped CNDs were developed by using citric acid as C source and urea as N sources as dopant for comparing (TEM images are presented in Fig. S5, Supporting Information). However, the phenomenon that surface interaction transforms the mixed liquid into sediment cannot be found (Fig. S6, Supporting Information). As we know, once a positive ion of PEDOT is fixed on the PSS polymer chain, the positive and negative ions in the vicinity of that one are also automatically approached to become an equilibrium state due to the spatial influence. However, it is like that a zipper is pulled up, the formed PEDOT:PSS poly-ion complex makes it very difficult to dissociate. Thus, the N doped CNDs cannot throw off the balance of PEDOT:PSS. By contrast, the uncombined sulfur double bond of S,N co-doped CNDs in Fig. 2i and Fig. S7 (Supporting Information) caused by thiourea introduces more microcosmic interstice inside the CNDs, accompanying with many thiourea residues are exposed on the surface. Special structure enhances CNDs activity, which is equipped to loot PSS by overcoming coulomb force between PEDOT and PSS and conjugate the sulfonic acid groups with amino. The TEM images of S, N co-doped CNDs after sustained electron bombarding are shown in Fig. 2j and k, and the collapsed internal structure proves the existence of microcosmic interstice. Photographs of the PEDOT:PSS:CNDs layered solution under UV (365 nm) illumination in Fig. S8 (Supporting Information) proved that CNDs still existed in the supernatants (Supplementary Movie 2). The flagging peak of Raman spectra in Fig. 2l also proved the internal variation in the system.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtener.2019.01.002.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Video 2
4
Video 2

The device structure and the cross-sectional scanning electron microscope (SEM) image are shown in Fig. 3 a, which reveals the total device thickness is around 600 nm (ITO 150 nm). The amplified device configuration in Fig. 3b demonstrates the cells have a planar configuration, where the total active layer thickness is approximately 300 nm. Fig. 3c illustrates the current density-voltage (J-V) curves of PSCs under AM 1.5 (inset: the photovoltaic parameters of the devices). In Fig. 3c, the PEDOT:PSS based devices have a J sc of 21.65 mAcm−2, an V oc of 0.98 V, and a FF of 72.05%, yielding a PCE of 15.41%, which is analogous with the published device (Table S1, Support information). By comparison, the modified PSCs exhibit a J sc of 22.60 mA cm−2, a V oc of 1.01 V, and a FF of 79.06%, leading to an optimized PCE of 18.03%. The incident photon to electron conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectrum of the PEDOT:PSS and PEDOT:PSS:CNDs devices is shown in Fig. S9 (Supporting Information). The PEDOT:PSS devices present a broad spectral response in the region of 300 nm–800 nm, an obvious improvement can be seen for the PEDOT:PSS:CNDs devices, which is coincident with the increased J sc.

The AFM morphology of PEDOT:PSS was first studied to account for the boosting photovoltaic performance. The lighter phase of images is attributed to PSS rich part, whereas the darker phase is PEDOT granules. The pristine PEDOT:PSS film in Fig. 3d shows uneven distribution with lots of PEDOT domains and entangled PSS part due to the exorbitant conglutination of ultrahigh molecular weight PSS. Adding small doses of CNDs into PEDOT:PSS (Fig. 3e) enhances the compatibility and dispersion within the PEDOT:PSS mixture through reducing coulombic attraction of the polymer molecule. The surface morphology (Fig. 3f) gradually changes from brindle-like to exquisite sponge-like with the further increased CNDs. The surface (Fig. 3g) becomes much smoother with much more uniform PEDOT and PSS distribution by adding 0.4 mg/mL. This optimized distribution and improved smoothness of PEDOT:PSS could decrease interfacial carrier trap and defect of PEDOT:PSS itself and improve the contact with CH3NH3PbI3, resulting in the balanced charge transfer inside the device [19,44]. The SEM and AFM images of CH3NH3PbI3 film on ITO/PEDOT:PSS in Fig. S10a (Supporting Information) shows a clustered-domain pattern. Densely interconnected crystalline strip, that contains many pin-holes and voids, looks relatively loose. By contrast, the CH3NH3PbI3 on the PEDOT:PSS:CNDs film (Fig. S10b) has a dense and flat surface morphology in the clustered-domain region. The perovskite crystals on PEDOT:PSS:CNDs are larger and more well-shaped. Hence, the modified PEDOT:PSS:CNDs changes the thermodynamic effect of the interface and makes surface more favorable for the crystal growing. The regulatory perovskite crystal growth introduces fewer grain boundaries of perovskite crystals, thus reducing the charge recombination and improving J sc and FF [45,46].

The dark J-V curves of device on semi-log coordinate is shown in Fig. 3h. It can be found that the current keeps in a low level in the shunt limited range of −0.8–0.8 V, increases exponentially with voltage in the diffusion/recombination regime (0.8–1.0 V), and saturates in the series resistance limited regime (1.0–1.3 V). As we know, the measured current in the shunt dominated range depends on the leakage currents caused by weak spots in the device. The current becomes diffusion/recombination dominated when the applied bias is more than built-in electric field. The measured shunt limited current for cells with the above-described film (Fig. 3g) presents a smaller level at reverse bias (-1 V to 0 V) and low positive bias (0 V–0.8 V), which corresponds to the improved coverage and the optimized contact between CH3NH3PbI3 grain and PEDOT:PSS films. These lessened weak spots trimmed the leakage currents in the device, and the good diode property in the dark is demonstrated, leading to the enhanced FF and J sc. The augment in the magnitude of V oc from 0.98 V to 1.01 V is not as obvious as that seen in FF, it is persuasive to ascribe the difference to the built-in electric field, which influence the built-in voltage inside the cells. The built-in voltage is the upper of V oc in the device, which can be obtained at the cross point in dark J-V curve between the exponential and SCLC regime under the flat band condition. The dark J-V curves of device under linear coordinate in Fig. S11 (Supporting Information) demonstrate the built-in electric field of modified cells is bigger than that of previous device, corresponding to the difference in V oc [47].

As mentioned above, the morphology of surface gradually becomes more uniform and more fine by adding small doses of CNDs into PEDOT:PSS. The charge carrier scattering in the film can be decreased (Fig. S12, Supporting Information), thus, the improved electron migration rate would increase device conductivity. The conductivity of the different films is illustrated in Fig. 3i. It can be seen that the conductivity increases firstly and then turns to decrease with the increased CNDs, arising a spike at 0.4 mg/mL. For low concentration of CNDs, the increased conductivity could improve the holes capture, which contributed to the photocurrent enhancement. However, as the CNDs continue to increase, a great mass of PSS was pillage from PEDOT, resulting in destroyed equilibrium systems with lots of PEDOT reunion. Additionally, the dramatically increased defect density and carrier scattering in the interface hinder the directional migration of carrier. Therefore, migration rate is great reduced, which reduces conductivity of films [48]. Time resolved PL (PL decay) behavior was surveyed to probe the influence of the defect and trap in PEDOT:PSS on carrier transfer. We consider the decay process to be the result of the quenching of photo-excited free carriers in the PEDOT:PSS domain through capturing by the defect and trap in PEDOT:PSS thin films. Fig. 3j shows the PL decay spectrum, and the lifetime was fitted with a bi-exponential decay function and summarized in the inset of Fig. 3j. For the pristine PEDOT:PSS, the fast-decay lifetime (τ 1) and slow-decay lifetime (τ 2) are 0.07 ns and 1.19 ns, respectively. The existence of CNDs (0.4 mg/mL) increases τ1 to 0.11 ns and τ 2 to 1.64 ns, respectively, suggesting that defects density inside the films is decreased. This also reveals the fact that PEDOT:PSS films should have better hole-extraction ability than that without CNDs.

The air stability of PSCs without encapsulation is exhibited in Fig. 4 a, the PEDOT:PSS-based device shows an inferior air stability in the atmosphere. While the device with PEDOT:PSS:CNDs shows the improved air stability. According to previous reports,15−18 the acidic nature of PEDOT:PSS causes corrosion to ITO electron, which is one of the important reason for the worse stability. By contrast, a large amount of amidogen on the surface of CNDs could neutralize hydrogen ions from PSS sour and lower the level of the acid, then prevent ITO corrosion. Meanwhile, the favorable ultraviolet light absorbing characteristic of CNDs could disorganize the CH3NH3PbI3 crystal degradation mechanism in a certain extent caused by the ultraviolet light, which can prevent device from aging. Compared with the PEDOT:PSS film, the UV transmittance of PEDOT:PSS:CNDs in Fig. 4b decreases in the region of 300–400 nm, which is in accordance with the CNDs absorption. Meanwhile, another important reason to cause low stability is the natural hygroscopic of PEDOT:PSS. The water contact angles of PEDOT:PSS and PEDOT:PSS:CNDs were evaluated to explore the hygroscopic of films. As shown in Fig. 4c–f, the increased water contact angles reveals the hydrophobic improvement, which means it protects against moisture from atmosphere, leading to a better air stability [49].

In summary, an easily prepared S, N co-doped CNDs was doped into PEDOT:PSS to achieve an efficient HTL for PSCs. Compared with PEDOT:PSS system, the functionalized CNDs can band the superfluous PSS and remove the bonding between PEDOT and PSS, resulting in an improved structural and electrical homogeneity. The optimized inner structure facilitates the hole extraction efficiency between the transporting interlayer and the perovskite absorber, yielding a relatively higher device performance of inverted CH3NH3PbI3-based PSCs. These results demonstrate that the functionalized design of CNDs can expand its application area in the optoelectronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to National Natural Science Foundation of China (61875072), the Special Project of the Province-University Co-constructing Program of Jilin Province (SXGJXX2017-3), the Science and Technology Innovation Leading Talent and Team Project of Jilin Province (20170519010JH), International Cooperation and Exchange Project of Jilin Province (20170414002GH, 20180414001GH), and Project of Graduate Innovation Fund of Jilin University (2017175, 2017170) for the support to the work.
